Exit
Alarum
Enter KING HENRY, EXETER, BEDFORD, GLOUCESTER, and soldiers, with scaling ladders

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more,
Or close the wall up with our English dead!
In peace there’s nothing so becomes a man
As modest stillness and humility,
But when the blast of war blows in our ears,
Then imitate the action of the tiger:
Stiffen the sinews, summon up the blood,
Disguise fair nature with hard-favored rage,
Then lend the eye a terrible aspect,
Let pry through the portage of the head
Like the brass cannon, let the brow o'erwhelm it
As fearfully as doth a gallèd rock
O'erhang and jutty his confounded base,
Swilled with the wild and wasteful ocean.
Now set the teeth and stretch the nostril wide,
Hold hard the breath and bend up every spirit
To his full height. On, on, you noblest English,
Whose blood is fet from fathers of war-proof,
Fathers that, like so many Alexanders,
Have in these parts from morn till even fought
And sheathed their swords for lack of argument.
Dishonor not your mothers. Now attest
That those whom you called fathers did beget you.
Be copy now to men of grosser blood,
And teach them how to war. And you, good yeoman,
Whose limbs were made in England, show us here
The mettle of your pasture. Let us swear
That you are worth your breeding, which I doubt not,
For there is none of you so mean and base
That hath not noble luster in your eyes.
I see you stand like greyhounds in the slips,
Straining upon the start. The game’s afoot.
Follow your spirit, and upon this charge
Cry “God for Harry, England, and Saint George!”

sentences.text:
Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more,
Or close the wall up with our English dead!
In peace there’s nothing so becomes a man
As modest stillness and humility,
But when the blast of war blows in our ears,
Then imitate the action of the tiger:
Stiffen the sinews, summon up the blood,
Disguise fair nature with hard-favored rage,
Then lend the eye a terrible aspect,
Let pry through the portage of the head
Like the brass cannon, let the brow o'erwhelm it
As fearfully as doth a gallèd rock
O'erhang and jutty his confounded base,
Swilled with the wild and wasteful ocean.
Now set the teeth and stretch the nostril wide,
Hold hard the breath and bend up every spirit
To his full height. On, on, you noblest English,
Whose blood is fet from fathers of war-proof,
Fathers that, like so many Alexanders,
Have in these parts from morn till even fought
And sheathed their swords for lack of argument.
Dishonor not your mothers. Now attest
That those whom you called fathers did beget you.
Be copy now to men of grosser blood,
And teach them how to war. And you, good yeoman,
Whose limbs were made in England, show us here
The mettle of your pasture. Let us swear
That you are worth your breeding, which I doubt not,
For there is none of you so mean and base
That hath not noble luster in your eyes.
I see you stand like greyhounds in the slips,
Straining upon the start. The game’s afoot.
Follow your spirit, and upon this charge
Cry “God for Harry, England, and Saint George!”

Alarum, and chambers go off. Exeunt
Alarum, and chambers go off. Exeunt
Enter NYM, BARDOLPH, PISTOL, and BOY

sentences.speaker: BARDOLPH
sentences.text: On, on, on, on, on! To the breach, to the breach!

sentences.speaker: BARDOLPH
sentences.text: On, on, on, on, on! To the breach, to the breach!

sentences.speaker: NYM
sentences.text:
Pray thee, corporal, stay. The knocks are too hot, and,
for mine own part, I have not a case of lives. The
humor of it is too hot; that is the very plainsong of
it.

sentences.speaker: NYM
sentences.text:

it.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
“The plainsong” is most just, for humors do abound.
[sings]
Knocks go and come. God’s vassals drop and die,
And sword and shield
In bloody field
Doth win immortal fame.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:

[sings]

And sword and shield

Doth win immortal fame.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
Would I were in an alehouse in London! I would give all
my fame for a pot of ale, and safety.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
Would I were in an alehouse in London! I would give all
my fame for a pot of ale, and safety.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
And I.
[sings]
If wishes would prevail with me,
My purpose should not fail with me,
But thither would I hie.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
[sings]
As duly,
But not as truly,
As bird doth sing on bough.
Enter FLUELLEN

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Up to the breach, you dogs! Avaunt, you cullions!

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Up to the breach, you dogs! Avaunt, you cullions!

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Be merciful, great duke, to men of mold. Abate thy
rage, abate thy manly rage, abate thy rage, great duke.
Good bawcock, 'bate thy rage. Use lenity, sweet chuck.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:

rage, abate thy manly rage, abate thy rage, great duke.
Good bawcock, 'bate thy rage. Use lenity, sweet chuck.

sentences.speaker: NYM
sentences.text: These be good humors. Your Honor wins bad humors.

sentences.speaker: NYM
sentences.text: These be good humors. Your Honor wins bad humors.
Exeunt all but BOY

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
As young as I am, I have observed these three swashers.
I am boy to them all three, but all they three, though
they would serve me, could not be man to me. For indeed
three such antics do not amount to a man: for Bardolph,
he is white-livered and red-faced, by the means whereof
he faces it out but fights not; for Pistol, he hath a
killing tongue and a quiet sword, by the means whereof
he breaks words and keeps whole weapons; for Nym, he
hath heard that men of few words are the best men, and
therefore he scorns to say his prayers, lest he should
be thought a coward, but his few bad words are matched
with as few good deeds, for he never broke any man’s
head but his own, and that was against a post when he
was drunk. They will steal anything and call it
purchase. Bardolph stole a lute case, bore it twelve
leagues, and sold it for three halfpence. Nym and
Bardolph are sworn brothers in filching, and in Calais
they stole a fire shovel. I knew by that piece of
service the men would carry coals. They would have me as
familiar with men’s pockets as their gloves or their
handkerchers, which makes much against my manhood, if I
should take from another’s pocket to put into mine, for
it is plain pocketing up of wrongs. I must leave them
and seek some better service. Their villainy goes
against my weak stomach, and therefore I must cast it
up.

it is plain pocketing up of wrongs. I must leave them
and seek some better service. Their villainy goes
against my weak stomach, and therefore I must cast it
up.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text:
As young as I am, I have observed these three swashers.
I am boy to them all three, but all they three, though
they would serve me, could not be man to me. For indeed
three such antics do not amount to a man: for Bardolph,
he is white-livered and red-faced, by the means whereof
he faces it out but fights not; for Pistol, he hath a
killing tongue and a quiet sword, by the means whereof
he breaks words and keeps whole weapons; for Nym, he
hath heard that men of few words are the best men, and
therefore he scorns to say his prayers, lest he should
be thought a coward, but his few bad words are matched
with as few good deeds, for he never broke any man’s
head but his own, and that was against a post when he
was drunk. They will steal anything and call it
purchase. Bardolph stole a lute case, bore it twelve
leagues, and sold it for three halfpence. Nym and
Bardolph are sworn brothers in filching, and in Calais
they stole a fire shovel. I knew by that piece of
service the men would carry coals. They would have me as
familiar with men’s pockets as their gloves or their
handkerchers, which makes much against my manhood, if I
should take from another’s pocket to put into mine, for
it is plain pocketing up of wrongs. I must leave them
and seek some better service. Their villainy goes
against my weak stomach, and therefore I must cast it
up.

Enter FLUELLEN and GOWER

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Captain Fluellen, you must come presently to the mines;
the duke of Gloucester would speak with you.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Captain Fluellen, you must come presently to the mines;
the duke of Gloucester would speak with you.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
To the mines? Tell you the duke it is not so good to
come to the mines, for, look you, the mines is not
according to the disciplines of the war. The concavities
of it is not sufficient, for, look you, th' athversary,
you may discuss unto the duke, look you, is digt
himself four yard under the countermines. By Cheshu, I
think he will plow up all if there is not better
directions.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
The duke of Gloucester, to whom the order of the siege
is given, is altogether directed by an Irishman, a very
valiant gentleman, i' faith.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: It is Captain Macmorris, is it not?

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: I think it be.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
By Cheshu, he is an ass, as in the world. I will verify
as much in his beard. He has no more directions in the
true disciplines of the wars, look you, of the Roman
disciplines, than is a puppy dog.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:

Enter Captain MACMORRIS and Captain JAMY

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Here he comes, and the Scots captain, Captain Jamy,
with him.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Captain Jamy is a marvelous falorous gentleman, that is
certain, and of great expedition and knowledge in
th'aunchient wars, upon my particular knowledge of his
directions. By Cheshu, he will maintain his argument as
well as any military man in the world in the disciplines
of the pristine wars of the Romans.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Captain Jamy is a marvelous falorous gentleman, that is
certain, and of great expedition and knowledge in
th'aunchient wars, upon my particular knowledge of his
directions. By Cheshu, he will maintain his argument as

of the pristine wars of the Romans.

sentences.speaker: JAMY
sentences.text: I say gudday, Captain Fluellen.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Godden to your Worship, good Captain James.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
How now, Captain Macmorris, have you quit the mines?
Have the pioneers given o'er?

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
How now, Captain Macmorris, have you quit the mines?
Have the pioneers given o'er?

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
By Chrish, la, ’tish ill done. The work ish give over.
The trompet sound the retreat. By my hand I swear, and
my father’s soul, the work ish ill done. It ish give
over. I would have blowed up the town, so Chrish save
me, la, in an hour. Oh, ’tish ill done, ’tish ill done,
by my hand, ’tish ill done.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Captain Macmorris, I beseech you now, will you voutsafe
me, look you, a few disputations with you as partly
touching or concerning the disciplines of the war, the
Roman wars? In the way of argument, look you, and
friendly communication, partly to satisfy my opinion,
and partly for the satisfaction, look you, of my mind,
as touching the direction of the military discipline,
that is the point.

sentences.speaker: JAMY
sentences.text:
It sall be vary gud, gud feith, gud captens bath, and I
sall quit you with gud leve, as I may pick occasion,
that sall I, marry.

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
It is no time to discourse, so Chrish save me. The day
is hot, and the weather, and the wars, and the king, and
the dukes. It is no time to discourse. The town is
beseeched, and the trumpet call us to the breach, and we
talk and, be Chrish, do nothing, ’tis shame for us all.
So God sa' me, ’tis shame to stand still. It is shame,
by my hand. And there is throats to be cut and works to
be done, and there ish nothing done, so Chrish sa' me,
la.

sentences.speaker: JAMY
sentences.text:
By the mess, ere theise eyes of mine take themselves to
slomber, ay’ll de gud service, or I’ll lig i' th' grund
for it, ay, or go to death. And I’ll pay ’t as
valorously as I may, that sall I suerly do, that is the
breff and the long. Marry, I wad full fain heard some
question ’tween you tway.

sentences.speaker: JAMY
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Captain Macmorris, I think, look you, under your
correction, there is not many of your nation—

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
Of my nation? What ish my nation? Ish a villain and a
basterd and a knave and a rascal. What ish my nation?
Who talks of my nation?

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
Of my nation? What ish my nation? Ish a villain and a
basterd and a knave and a rascal. What ish my nation?
Who talks of my nation?

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
Of my nation? What ish my nation? Ish a villain and a
basterd and a knave and a rascal. What ish my nation?

sentences.text:
Of my nation? What ish my nation? Ish a villain and a

Who talks of my nation?

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
Of my nation? What ish my nation? Ish a villain and a
basterd and a knave and a rascal. What ish my nation?
Who talks of my nation?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Look you, if you take the matter otherwise than is
meant, Captain Macmorris, peradventure I shall think you
do not use me with that affability as, in discretion,
you ought to use me, look you, being as good a man as
yourself, both in the disciplines of war and in the
derivation of my birth and in other particularities.

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
I do not know you so good a man as myself. So Chrish
save me, I will cut off your head.

sentences.speaker: MACMORRIS
sentences.text:
I do not know you so good a man as myself. So Chrish
save me, I will cut off your head.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: Gentlemen both, you will mistake each other.

sentences.speaker: JAMY
sentences.text: Ah, that’s a foul fault.
A parley sounds

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: The town sounds a parley.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Captain Macmorris, when there is more better
opportunity to be required, look you, I will be so bold
as to tell you I know the disciplines of war, and there
is an end.
Exeunt
Enter the GOVERNOR and some citizens on the walls. Enter KING HENRY and his train before the gates
Enter the GOVERNOR and some citizens on the walls. Enter KING HENRY and his train before the gates

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
How yet resolves the governor of the town?
This is the latest parle we will admit.
Therefore to our best mercy give yourselves
Or, like to men proud of destruction,
Defy us to our worst. For, as I am a soldier,
A name that in my thoughts becomes me best,
If I begin the batt'ry once again,
I will not leave the half-achieved Harfleur
Till in her ashes she lie burièd.
The gates of mercy shall be all shut up,
And the fleshed soldier, rough and hard of heart,
In liberty of bloody hand, shall range
With conscience wide as hell, mowing like grass
Your fresh fair virgins and your flow'ring infants.
What is it then to me if impious war,
Arrayed in flames like to the prince of fiends,
Do with his smirched complexion all fell feats
Enlinked to waste and desolation?
What is ’t to me, when you yourselves are cause,
If your pure maidens fall into the hand
Of hot and forcing violation?
What rein can hold licentious wickedness
When down the hill he holds his fierce career?
We may as bootless spend our vain command
Upon th' enragèd soldiers in their spoil
As send precepts to the Leviathan
To come ashore. Therefore, you men of Harfleur,
Take pity of your town and of your people
Whiles yet my soldiers are in my command,
Whiles yet the cool and temperate wind of grace
O'erblows the filthy and contagious clouds
Of heady murder, spoil, and villainy.
If not, why, in a moment look to see
The blind and bloody soldier with foul hand
Desire the locks of your shrill-shrieking daughters,
Your fathers taken by the silver beards
And their most reverend heads dashed to the walls,
Your naked infants spitted upon pikes
Whiles the mad mothers with their howls confused
Do break the clouds, as did the wives of Jewry
At Herod’s bloody-hunting slaughtermen.
What say you? Will you yield and this avoid
Or, guilty in defense, be thus destroyed?

The gates of mercy shall be all shut up,
And the fleshed soldier, rough and hard of heart,
In liberty of bloody hand, shall range
With conscience wide as hell, mowing like grass
Your fresh fair virgins and your flow'ring infants.
What is it then to me if impious war,
Arrayed in flames like to the prince of fiends,
Do with his smirched complexion all fell feats
Enlinked to waste and desolation?
What is ’t to me, when you yourselves are cause,
If your pure maidens fall into the hand
Of hot and forcing violation?
What rein can hold licentious wickedness
When down the hill he holds his fierce career?
We may as bootless spend our vain command
Upon th' enragèd soldiers in their spoil
As send precepts to the Leviathan
To come ashore. Therefore, you men of Harfleur,
Take pity of your town and of your people
Whiles yet my soldiers are in my command,
Whiles yet the cool and temperate wind of grace
O'erblows the filthy and contagious clouds
Of heady murder, spoil, and villainy.
If not, why, in a moment look to see
The blind and bloody soldier with foul hand
Desire the locks of your shrill-shrieking daughters,
Your fathers taken by the silver beards
And their most reverend heads dashed to the walls,
Your naked infants spitted upon pikes
Whiles the mad mothers with their howls confused
Do break the clouds, as did the wives of Jewry
At Herod’s bloody-hunting slaughtermen.
What say you? Will you yield and this avoid
Or, guilty in defense, be thus destroyed?

sentences.speaker: GOVERNOR
sentences.text:
Our expectation hath this day an end.
The Dauphin, whom of succors we entreated,
Returns us that his powers are yet not ready
To raise so great a siege. Therefore, great King,
We yield our town and lives to thy soft mercy.
Enter our gates, dispose of us and ours,
For we no longer are defensible.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Open your gates. [Exit Governor]
Come, uncle Exeter,
Go you and enter Harfleur. There remain
And fortify it strongly 'gainst the French.
Use mercy to them all. For us, dear uncle,
The winter coming on and sickness growing
Upon our soldiers, we will retire to Calais.
Tonight in Harfleur will we be your guest;
Tomorrow for the march are we addressed.

Flourish, and enter the town
Enter KATHERINE and ALICE

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Alice, tu as été en Angleterre, et tu parles bien le
langage.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Un peu, madame .

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Je te prie, m'enseignez. Il faut que j'apprenne à
parler .
Comment appelez-vous la main en anglais?

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Je te prie, m'enseignez. Il faut que j'apprenne à
parler .
Comment appelez-vous la main en anglais?

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Je te prie, m'enseignez. Il faut que j'apprenne à
parler .
Comment appelez-vous la main en anglais?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: La main? Elle est appelée “de hand.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: La main? Elle est appelée “de hand.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: “De hand.” Et les doigts?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text:
Les doigts? Ma foi, j'oublie les doigts; mais je me
souviendrai. Les doigts? Je pense qu'ils sont appelés
“de fingres”; oui, “de fingres.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text:
Les doigts? Ma foi, j'oublie les doigts; mais je me
souviendrai. Les doigts? Je pense qu'ils sont appelés
“de fingres”; oui, “de fingres.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text:

“de fingres”; oui, “de fingres.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE

“de fingres”; oui, “de fingres.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
La main , “de hand”; les doigts , “de fingres.” Je pense
que je suis le bon écolier. J'ai gagné deux mots
d'anglais vitement. Comment appelez-vous les ongles?

d'anglais vitement. Comment appelez-vous les ongles?

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
La main , “de hand”; les doigts , “de fingres.” Je pense
que je suis le bon écolier. J'ai gagné deux mots
d'anglais vitement. Comment appelez-vous les ongles?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Les ongles? Nous les appelons “de nails.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Les ongles? Nous les appelons “de nails.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
“De nails.” Écoutez. Dites-moi si je parle bien : “de
hand, de fingres, et de nails.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
“De nails.” Écoutez. Dites-moi si je parle bien : “de
hand, de fingres, et de nails.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
“De nails.” Écoutez. Dites-moi si je parle bien : “de
hand, de fingres, et de nails.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: C'est bien dit, madame. Il est fort bon anglais.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: C'est bien dit, madame. Il est fort bon anglais.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: Dites-moi l'anglais pour le bras.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “De arme,” madame.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: Et le coude?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “D'elbow.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
“D'elbow.” Je m'en fais la répétition de tous les mots
que vous m'avez appris dés à présent.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Il est trop difficile, madame, comme je pense.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Excusez-moi, Alice. Écoutez: “de hand, de fingres, de
nails, de arma, de bilbow.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Excusez-moi, Alice. Écoutez: “de hand, de fingres, de
nails, de arma, de bilbow.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “D'elbow,” madame.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Ô Seigneur Dieu! Je m'en oublie; “d'elbow.” Comment
appelez-vous le col?

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Ô Seigneur Dieu! Je m'en oublie; “d'elbow.” Comment
appelez-vous le col?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “De nick,” madame.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: “De nick.” Et le menton?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “De chin.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: “De sin.” Le col, “de nick”; le menton, “de sin.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text:
Oui. Sauf votre honneur, en vérité, vous prononcez les
mots aussi droit que les natifs d'Angleterre.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text:
Oui. Sauf votre honneur, en vérité, vous prononcez les
mots aussi droit que les natifs d'Angleterre.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Je ne doute point d'apprendre, par la grâce de Dieu, et
en peu de temps.

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: N'avez vous pas déjà oublié ce que je vous ai enseigné?

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Non, je réciterai à vous promptement: “de hand, de
fingre, de nails—”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “De nails,” madame.

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: “De nails, de arme, de ilbow.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Sauf votre honneur, “d'elbow.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
Ainsi dis-je: “d'elbow, de nick, et de sin.” Comment
appelez-vous le pied et la robe?

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “Le foot,” madame, et “le count.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: “Le foot,” madame, et “le count.”

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text:
“ Le foot” et “de count.” Ô Seigneur Dieu! Ils sont mots
de son mauvais, corruptible, gros, et impudique, et non
pour les dames d'honneur d'user. Je ne voudrais
prononcer ces mots devant les seigneurs de France pour
tout le monde. Foh! “Le foot” et “le count”! Néanmoins,
je réciterai une autre fois ma leçon ensemble: “d' hand,
de fingre, de nails, d' arme, d'elbow, de nick, de sin,
de foot, le count.”

sentences.speaker: ALICE
sentences.text: Excellent, madame!

sentences.speaker: KATHERINE
sentences.text: C'est assez pour une fois: Allons-nous à dîner.
Exeunt
Enter the KING OF FRANCE, the DAUPHIN, the duke of BOURBON, the CONSTABLE of France, and others

sentences.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
sentences.text: 'Tis certain he hath passed the river Somme.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
An if he be not fought withal, my lord,
Let us not live in France. Let us quit all
And give our vineyards to a barbarous people.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
An if he be not fought withal, my lord,
Let us not live in France. Let us quit all
And give our vineyards to a barbarous people.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Ô Dieu vivant , shall a few sprays of us,
The emptying of our fathers' luxury,
Our scions, put in wild and savage stock,
Spurt up so suddenly into the clouds
And overlook their grafters?

sentences.speaker: BOURBON
sentences.text:
Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards!
Mort de ma vie, if they march along
Unfought withal, but I will sell my dukedom
To buy a slobb'ry and a dirty farm
In that nook-shotten isle of Albion.

sentences.speaker: BOURBON
sentences.text:
Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards!
Mort de ma vie, if they march along
Unfought withal, but I will sell my dukedom
To buy a slobb'ry and a dirty farm
In that nook-shotten isle of Albion.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
Dieu de batailles, where have they this mettle?
Is not their climate foggy, raw, and dull,
On whom, as in despite, the sun looks pale,
Killing their fruit with frowns? Can sodden water,
A drench for sur-reined jades, their barley broth,
Decoct their cold blood to such valiant heat?
And shall our quick blood, spirited with wine,
Seem frosty? Oh, for honor of our land,
Let us not hang like roping icicles
Upon our houses' thatch, whiles a more frosty people
Sweat drops of gallant youth in our rich fields!
“Poor” we may call them in their native lords.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
By faith and honor,
Our madams mock at us and plainly say
Our mettle is bred out and they will give
Their bodies to the lust of English youth
To new-store France with bastard warriors.

sentences.speaker: BOURBON
sentences.text:
And teach lavoltas high, and swift corantos,
Saying our grace is only in our heels
And that we are most lofty runaways.
They bid us to the
English dancing schools.

sentences.speaker: BOURBON
sentences.text:
And teach lavoltas high, and swift corantos,
Saying our grace is only in our heels

They bid us to the
English dancing schools.

sentences.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
sentences.text:
Where is Montjoy the herald? Speed him hence.
Let him greet England with our sharp defiance.
Up, princes, and, with spirit of honor edged
More sharper than your swords, hie to the field:
Charles Delabreth, High Constable of France;
You dukes of Orléans, Bourbon, and of Berri,
Alençon, Brabant, Bar, and Burgundy;
Jacques Chatillon, Rambures, Vaudemont,
Beaumont, Grandpré, Roussi, and Faulconbridge,
Foix, Lestrale, Bouciqualt, and Charolois;
High dukes, great princes, barons, lords, and knights,
For your great seats now quit you of great shames.
Bar Harry England, that sweeps through our land
With pennons painted in the blood of Harfleur.
Rush on his host, as doth the melted snow
Upon the valleys, whose low vassal seat
The Alps doth spit and void his rheum upon.
Go down upon him—you have power enough—
And in a captive chariot into Rouen
Bring him our prisoner.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
This becomes the great!
Sorry am I his numbers are so few,
His soldiers sick and famished in their march ,
For, I am sure, when he shall see our army
He’ll drop his heart into the sink of fear
And for achievement offer us his ransom.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
sentences.text:
Therefore, Lord Constable, haste on Montjoy
And let him say to England that we send
To know what willing ransom he will give.
—Prince Dauphin, you shall stay with us in Rouen.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text: Not so, I do beseech your Majesty.

sentences.speaker: KING OF FRANCE
sentences.text:
Be patient, for you shall remain with us.
—Now forth, Lord Constable and princes all,
And quickly bring us word of England’s fall.
Exeunt
Enter GOWER and FLUELLEN, meeting

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: How now, Captain Fluellen? Come you from the bridge?

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: How now, Captain Fluellen? Come you from the bridge?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
I assure you, there is very excellent services
committed at the bridge.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: Is the duke of Exeter safe?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
The duke of Exeter is as magnanimous as Agamemnon, and
a man that I love and honor with my soul and my heart
and my duty and my life and my living and my uttermost
power. He is not, God be praised and blessed, any hurt
in the world, but keeps the bridge most valiantly, with
excellent discipline. There is an aunchient lieutenant
there at the pridge. I think in my very conscience he is
as valiant a man as Mark Antony, and he is a man of no
estimation in the world, but I did see him do as gallant
service.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: What do you call him?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: He is called Aunchient Pistol.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: I know him not.
Enter PISTOL

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Here is the man.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Captain, I thee beseech to do me favors.
The duke of Exeter doth love thee well.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Captain, I thee beseech to do me favors.
The duke of Exeter doth love thee well.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Ay, I praise God, and I have merited some love at his
hands.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Bardolph, a soldier firm and sound of heart
And of buxom valor, hath, by cruel Fate
And giddy Fortune’s furious fickle wheel,
That goddess blind
That stands upon the rolling restless stone—

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
By your patience, Aunchient Pistol, Fortune is painted
blind, with a muffler afore her eyes, to signify to you
that Fortune is blind; and she is painted also with a
wheel to signify to you, which is the moral of it, that
she is turning and inconstant, and mutability and
variation; and her foot, look you, is fixed upon a
spherical stone, which rolls and rolls and rolls. In
good truth, the poet makes a most excellent description
of it. Fortune is an excellent moral.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Fortune is Bardolph’s foe and frowns on him,
For he hath stolen a pax and hangèd must he be.
A damnèd death!
Let gallows gape for dog, let man go free,
And let not hemp his windpipe suffocate.
But Exeter hath given the doom of death
For pax of little price.
Therefore go speak—the duke will hear thy voice—
And let not Bardolph’s vital thread be cut
With edge of penny cord and vile reproach.
Speak, Captain, for his life, and I will thee requite.

sentences.text:
Fortune is Bardolph’s foe and frowns on him,
For he hath stolen a pax and hangèd must he be.
A damnèd death!
Let gallows gape for dog, let man go free,
And let not hemp his windpipe suffocate.
But Exeter hath given the doom of death
For pax of little price.
Therefore go speak—the duke will hear thy voice—
And let not Bardolph’s vital thread be cut
With edge of penny cord and vile reproach.
Speak, Captain, for his life, and I will thee requite.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Aunchient Pistol, I do partly understand your meaning.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Why then, rejoice therefore.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Certainly, aunchient, it is not a thing to rejoice at,
for if, look you, he were my brother, I would desire the
duke to use his good pleasure and put him to execution,
for discipline ought to be used.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Die and be damned, and figo for thy friendship!

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: It is well.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: The fig of Spain!
Exit

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: Very good.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Why, this is an arrant counterfeit rascal. I remember
him now, a bawd, a cutpurse.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Why, this is an arrant counterfeit rascal. I remember
him now, a bawd, a cutpurse.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
I’ll assure you, he uttered as prave words at the
pridge as you shall see in a summer’s day. But it is
very well; what he has spoke to me, that is well, I
warrant you, when time is serve.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
I’ll assure you, he uttered as prave words at the
pridge as you shall see in a summer’s day. But it is
very well; what he has spoke to me, that is well, I

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text:
Why, ’tis a gull, a fool, a rogue, that now and then
goes to the wars to grace himself at his return into
London under the form of a soldier. And such fellows are
perfect in the great commanders' names, and they will
learn you by rote where services were done —at such and
such a sconce, at such a breach, at such a convoy; who
came off bravely, who was shot, who disgraced, what
terms the enemy stood on. And this they con perfectly in
the phrase of war, which they trick up with new-tuned
oaths; and what a beard of the general’s cut and a
horrid suit of the camp will do among foaming bottles
and ale-washed wits is wonderful to be thought on. But
you must learn to know such slanders of the age, or else
you may be marvelously mistook.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
I tell you what, Captain Gower. I do perceive he is not
the man that he would gladly make show to the world he
is. If I find a hole in his coat, I will tell him my
mind.

Drum and colors Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and soldiers
Drum and colors Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and soldiers

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Hark you, the king is coming, and I must speak with him
from the pridge.—God pless your Majesty.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: How now, Fluellen, cam’st thou from the bridge?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
Ay, so please your Majesty. The duke of Exeter has very
gallantly maintained the pridge. The French is gone
off, look you, and there is gallant and most prave
passages. Marry, th' athversary was have possession of
the pridge, but he is enforced to retire, and the duke
of Exeter is master of the pridge. I can tell your
Majesty, the duke is a prave man.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: What men have you lost, Fluellen?

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
The perdition of th' athversary hath been very great,
reasonable great. Marry, for my part, I think the duke
hath lost never a man, but one that is like to be
executed for robbing a church, one Bardolph, if your
Majesty know the man. His face is all bubukles and
whelks and knobs and flames o' fire; and his lips blows
at his nose, and it is like a coal of fire, sometimes
plue and sometimes red, but his nose is executed, and
his fire’s out.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
We would have all such offenders so cut off, and we
give express charge that in our marches through the
country there be nothing compelled from the villages,
nothing taken but paid for, none of the French upbraided
or abused in disdainful language; for when lenity and
cruelty play for a kingdom, the gentler gamester is the
soonest winner.

Tucket Enter MONTJOY
Tucket Enter MONTJOY

sentences.speaker: MONTJOY
sentences.text: You know me by my habit.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Well then, I know thee. What shall I know of thee?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Well then, I know thee. What shall I know of thee?

sentences.speaker: MONTJOY
sentences.text: My master’s mind.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Unfold it.

sentences.speaker: MONTJOY
sentences.text:
Thus says my king: “Say thou to Harry of England,
though we seemed dead, we did but sleep. Advantage is a
better soldier than rashness. Tell him we could have
rebuked him at Harfleur, but that we thought not good to
bruise an injury till it were full ripe. Now we speak
upon our cue, and our voice is imperial. England shall
repent his folly, see his weakness, and admire our
sufferance. Bid him therefore consider of his ransom,
which must proportion the losses we have borne, the
subjects we have lost, the disgrace we have digested,
which, in weight to reanswer, his pettiness would bow
under. For our losses, his exchequer is too poor; for
th' effusion of our blood, the muster of his kingdom too
faint a number; and for our disgrace, his own person,
kneeling at our feet but a weak and worthless
satisfaction. To this, add defiance, and tell him, for
conclusion, he hath betrayed his followers, whose
condemnation is pronounced.” So far my king and master;
so much my office.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: What is thy name? I know thy quality.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: What is thy name? I know thy quality.

sentences.speaker: MONTJOY
sentences.text: Montjoy.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Thou dost thy office fairly. Turn thee back,
And tell thy king I do not seek him now
But could be willing to march on to Calais
Without impeachment, for, to say the sooth,
Though ’tis no wisdom to confess so much
Unto an enemy of craft and vantage,
My people are with sickness much enfeebled,
My numbers lessened, and those few I have
Almost no better than so many French,
Who when they were in health, I tell thee, herald,
I thought upon one pair of English legs
Did march three Frenchmen. Yet, forgive me, God,
That I do brag thus. This your air of France
Hath blown that vice in me. I must repent.
Go therefore, tell thy master: here I am.
My ransom is this frail and worthless trunk,
My army but a weak and sickly guard,
Yet, God before, tell him we will come on
Though France himself and such another neighbor
Stand in our way. There’s for thy labor, Montjoy.
Go bid thy master well advise himself:
If we may pass, we will; if we be hindered,
We shall your tawny ground with your red blood
Discolor. And so, Montjoy, fare you well.
The sum of all our answer is but this:
We would not seek a battle as we are,
Nor, as we are, we say we will not shun it.
So tell your master.

My numbers lessened, and those few I have
Almost no better than so many French,
Who when they were in health, I tell thee, herald,
I thought upon one pair of English legs
Did march three Frenchmen. Yet, forgive me, God,
That I do brag thus. This your air of France
Hath blown that vice in me. I must repent.
Go therefore, tell thy master: here I am.
My ransom is this frail and worthless trunk,
My army but a weak and sickly guard,
Yet, God before, tell him we will come on
Though France himself and such another neighbor
Stand in our way. There’s for thy labor, Montjoy.
Go bid thy master well advise himself:
If we may pass, we will; if we be hindered,
We shall your tawny ground with your red blood
Discolor. And so, Montjoy, fare you well.
The sum of all our answer is but this:
We would not seek a battle as we are,
Nor, as we are, we say we will not shun it.
So tell your master.

sentences.speaker: MONTJOY

Exit

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: I hope they will not come upon us now.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
We are in God’s hand, brother, not in theirs.
March to the bridge. It now draws toward night.
Beyond the river we’ll encamp ourselves,
And on tomorrow bid them march away.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN

My lord of Orléans, and my Lord High Constable, you
talk of horse and armor?

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN

My lord of Orléans, and my Lord High Constable, you

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
What a long night is this! I will not change my horse
with any that treads but on four pasterns. Çà ha! He
bounds from the earth, as if his entrails were hairs, le
cheval volant , the Pegasus, qui a les narines de feu .
When I bestride him, I soar; I am a hawk; he trots the
air. The earth sings when he touches it. The basest horn
of his hoof is more musical than the pipe of Hermes.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
And of the heat of the ginger. It is a beast for
Perseus. He is pure air and fire; and the dull elements
of earth and water never appear in him, but only in
patient stillness while his rider mounts him. He is
indeed a horse, and all other jades you may call beasts.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
It is the prince of palfreys. His neigh is like the
bidding of a monarch, and his countenance enforces
homage.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Nay, the man hath no wit that cannot, from the rising
of the lark to the lodging of the lamb, vary deserved
praise on my palfrey. It is a theme as fluent as the
sea. Turn the sands into eloquent tongues, and my horse
is argument for them all. 'Tis a subject for a sovereign
to reason on, and for a sovereign’s sovereign to ride
on, and for the world, familiar to us and unknown, to
lay apart their particular functions and wonder at him.
I once writ a sonnet in his praise and began thus:
“Wonder of nature—”

Me well—which is the prescript praise and perfection of
a good and particular mistress.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
Nay, for methought yesterday your mistress shrewdly
shook your back.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text: So perhaps did yours.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: Mine was not bridled.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Oh, then belike she was old and gentle, and you rode,
like a kern of Ireland, your French hose off and in your
straight strossers.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: You have good judgment in horsemanship.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Be warned by me, then: they that ride so, and ride not
warily, fall into foul bogs. I had rather have my horse
to my mistress.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Be warned by me, then: they that ride so, and ride not
warily, fall into foul bogs. I had rather have my horse
to my mistress.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: I had as lief have my mistress a jade.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text: I tell thee, Constable, my mistress wears his own hair.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
I could make as true a boast as that if I had a sow to
my mistress.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
“Le chien est retourné à son propre vomissement, et la
truie lavée au bourbier.” Thou mak’st use of anything.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
“Le chien est retourné à son propre vomissement, et la
truie lavée au bourbier.” Thou mak’st use of anything.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
Yet do I not use my horse for my mistress, or any such
proverb so little kin to the purpose.

sentences.speaker: RAMBURES
sentences.text:
My Lord Constable, the armor that I saw in your tent
tonight, are those stars or suns upon it?

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: Stars, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text: Some of them will fall tomorrow, I hope.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: And yet my sky shall not want.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
That may be, for you bear a many superfluously, and
’twere more honor some were away.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
Ev'n as your horse bears your praises—who would trot as
well were some of your brags dismounted.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Would I were able to load him with his desert! Will it
never be day? I will trot tomorrow a mile, and my way
shall be paved with English faces.

way. But I would it were morning, for I would fain be
about the ears of the English.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN

My Lord High Constable, the English lie within fifteen
hundred paces of your tents.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: Who hath measured the ground?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: The Lord Grandpré.

Alas, poor Harry of England! He longs not for the
dawning as we do.

Just, just; and the men do sympathize with the mastiffs
in robustious and rough coming on, leaving their wits
with their wives. And then give them great meals of beef
and iron and steel, they will eat like wolves and fight
like devils.

shall we about it?

Enter CHORUS

sentences.speaker: CHORUS
sentences.text:
Now entertain conjecture of a time
When creeping murmur and the poring dark
Fills the wide vessel of the universe.
From camp to camp, through the foul womb of night,
The hum of either army stilly sounds,
That the fixed sentinels almost receive
The secret whispers of each other’s watch.
Fire answers fire, and through their paly flames
Each battle sees the other’s umbered face.
Steed threatens steed, in high and boastful neighs
Piercing the night’s dull ear; and from the tents
The armorers, accomplishing the knights,
With busy hammers closing rivets up,
Give dreadful note of preparation.
The country cocks do crow, the clocks do toll,
And, the third hour of drowsy morning named,
Proud of their numbers and secure in soul,
The confident and overlusty French
Do the low-rated English play at dice
And chide the cripple, tardy-gaited night,
Who like a foul and ugly witch doth limp
So tediously away. The poor condemnèd English,
Like sacrifices, by their watchful fires
Sit patiently and inly ruminate
The morning’s danger; and their gesture sad,
Investing lank-lean cheeks and war-worn coats,
Presenteth them unto the gazing moon
So many horrid ghosts. Oh, now, who will behold
The royal captain of this ruined band
Walking from watch to watch, from tent to tent?
Let him cry, “Praise and glory on his head!”
For forth he goes and visits all his host,
Bids them good morrow with a modest smile,
And calls them brothers, friends, and countrymen.
Upon his royal face there is no note
How dread an army hath enrounded him,
Nor doth he dedicate one jot of color
Unto the weary and all-watchèd night,
But freshly looks and overbears attaint
With cheerful semblance and sweet majesty,
That every wretch, pining and pale before,
Beholding him, plucks comfort from his looks.
A largess universal, like the sun,
His liberal eye doth give to everyone,
Thawing cold fear, that mean and gentle all
Behold, as may unworthiness define,
A little touch of Harry in the night.
And so our scene must to the battle fly,
Where, Oh, for pity, we shall much disgrace,
With four or five most vile and ragged foils
Right ill-disposed in brawl ridiculous,
The name of Agincourt. Yet sit and see,
Minding true things by what their mock'ries be.

From camp to camp, through the foul womb of night,
The hum of either army stilly sounds,
That the fixed sentinels almost receive
The secret whispers of each other’s watch.
Fire answers fire, and through their paly flames
Each battle sees the other’s umbered face.
Steed threatens steed, in high and boastful neighs
Piercing the night’s dull ear; and from the tents
The armorers, accomplishing the knights,
With busy hammers closing rivets up,
Give dreadful note of preparation.
The country cocks do crow, the clocks do toll,
And, the third hour of drowsy morning named,
Proud of their numbers and secure in soul,
The confident and overlusty French
Do the low-rated English play at dice
And chide the cripple, tardy-gaited night,
Who like a foul and ugly witch doth limp
So tediously away. The poor condemnèd English,
Like sacrifices, by their watchful fires
Sit patiently and inly ruminate
The morning’s danger; and their gesture sad,
Investing lank-lean cheeks and war-worn coats,
Presenteth them unto the gazing moon
So many horrid ghosts. Oh, now, who will behold
The royal captain of this ruined band
Walking from watch to watch, from tent to tent?
Let him cry, “Praise and glory on his head!”
For forth he goes and visits all his host,
Bids them good morrow with a modest smile,
And calls them brothers, friends, and countrymen.
Upon his royal face there is no note
How dread an army hath enrounded him,
Nor doth he dedicate one jot of color
Unto the weary and all-watchèd night,
But freshly looks and overbears attaint
With cheerful semblance and sweet majesty,
That every wretch, pining and pale before,
Beholding him, plucks comfort from his looks.
A largess universal, like the sun,
His liberal eye doth give to everyone,
Thawing cold fear, that mean and gentle all
Behold, as may unworthiness define,
A little touch of Harry in the night.
And so our scene must to the battle fly,
Where, Oh, for pity, we shall much disgrace,
With four or five most vile and ragged foils
Right ill-disposed in brawl ridiculous,
The name of Agincourt. Yet sit and see,
Minding true things by what their mock'ries be.

Enter KING HENRY, BEDFORD, and GLOUCESTER

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Gloucester, ’tis true that we are in great danger.
The greater therefore should our courage be.
—Good morrow, brother Bedford. God almighty,
There is some soul of goodness in things evil,
Would men observingly distill it out.
For our bad neighbor makes us early stirrers,
Which is both healthful and good husbandry.
Besides, they are our outward consciences
And preachers to us all, admonishing
That we should dress us fairly for our end.
Thus may we gather honey from the weed
And make a moral of the devil himself.

Enter ERPINGHAM

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Good morrow, old Sir Thomas Erpingham.
A good soft pillow for that good white head
Were better than a churlish turf of France.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Good morrow, old Sir Thomas Erpingham.
A good soft pillow for that good white head
Were better than a churlish turf of France.

sentences.speaker: ERPINGHAM
sentences.text:
Not so, my liege, this lodging likes me better,
Since I may say, “Now lie I like a king.”

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
'Tis good for men to love their present pains
Upon example. So the spirit is eased.
And when the mind is quickened, out of doubt,
The organs, though defunct and dead before,
Break up their drowsy grave and newly move,
With casted slough and fresh legerity.
Lend me thy cloak, Sir Thomas. Brothers both,
Commend me to the princes in our camp,
Do my good morrow to them, and anon
Desire them all to my pavilion.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: We shall, my liege.

sentences.speaker: ERPINGHAM
sentences.text: Shall I attend your Grace?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
No, my good knight.
Go with my brothers to my lords of England.
I and my bosom must debate awhile,
And then I would no other company.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ERPINGHAM
sentences.text: The Lord in heaven bless thee, noble Harry!
Exeunt all but KING HENRY

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: God-a-mercy, old heart, thou speak’st cheerfully.
Enter PISTOL

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Qui vous là?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: A friend.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Discuss unto me: art thou officer or art thou base,
common, and popular?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: I am a gentleman of a company.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Trail’st thou the puissant pike?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Even so. What are you?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Even so. What are you?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: As good a gentleman as the emperor.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Then you are a better than the king.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
The king’s a bawcock, and a heart of gold,
A lad of life, an imp of fame,
Of parents good, of fist most valiant.
I kiss his dirty shoe, and from heartstring
I love the lovely bully. What is thy name?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:

Of parents good, of fist most valiant.
I kiss his dirty shoe, and from heartstring
I love the lovely bully. What is thy name?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Harry le Roy.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Le Roy? A Cornish name. Art thou of Cornish crew?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Le Roy? A Cornish name. Art thou of Cornish crew?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Le Roy? A Cornish name. Art thou of Cornish crew?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: No, I am a Welshman.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Know’st thou Fluellen?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Yes.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Tell him I’ll knock his leek about his pate
Upon Saint Davy’s day.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Do not you wear your dagger in your cap that day, lest
he knock that about yours.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Art thou his friend?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: And his kinsman too.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: The figo for thee then!

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: I thank you. God be with you.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: I thank you. God be with you.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: My name is Pistol called.
Exit

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: It sorts well with your fierceness.
Enter FLUELLEN and GOWER

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: Captain Fluellen.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
So. In the name of Jesu Christ, speak fewer. It is the
greatest admiration in the universal world when the true
and aunchient prerogatifes and laws of the wars is not
kept. If you would take the pains but to examine the
wars of Pompey the Great, you shall find, I warrant you,
that there is no tiddle toddle nor pibble babble in
Pompey’s camp. I warrant you, you shall find the
ceremonies of the wars and the cares of it and the forms
of it and the sobriety of it and the modesty of it to
be otherwise.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: Why, the enemy is loud. You hear him all night.

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: Why, the enemy is loud. You hear him all night.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text:
If the enemy is an ass and a fool and a prating
coxcomb, is it meet, think you, that we should also,
look you, be an ass and a fool and a prating coxcomb, in
your own conscience, now?

sentences.speaker: GOWER
sentences.text: I will speak lower.

sentences.speaker: FLUELLEN
sentences.text: I pray you and beseech you that you will.
Exeunt GOWER and FLUELLEN

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Though it appear a little out of fashion,
There is much care and valor in this Welshman.
Enter three soldiers, John BATES, Alexander COURT, and Michael WILLIAMS

sentences.speaker: COURT
sentences.text:
Brother John Bates, is not that the morning which
breaks yonder?

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
I think it be, but we have no great cause to desire the
approach of day.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
We see yonder the beginning of the day, but I think we
shall never see the end of it.—Who goes there?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: A friend.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Under what captain serve you?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: Under Sir Thomas Erpingham.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
A good old commander and a most kind gentleman. I pray
you, what thinks he of our estate?

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
A good old commander and a most kind gentleman. I pray
you, what thinks he of our estate?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Even as men wracked upon a sand, that look to be washed
off the next tide.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text: He hath not told his thought to the king?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
No. Nor it is not meet he should, for, though I speak
it to you, I think the king is but a man as I am. The
violet smells to him as it doth to me. The element shows
to him as it doth to me. All his senses have but human
conditions. His ceremonies laid by, in his nakedness he
appears but a man, and though his affections are higher
mounted than ours, yet when they stoop, they stoop with
the like wing. Therefore, when he sees reason of fears
as we do, his fears, out of doubt, be of the same relish
as ours are. Yet, in reason, no man should possess him
with any appearance of fear, lest he, by showing it,
should dishearten his army.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
He may show what outward courage he will, but I
believe, as cold a night as ’tis, he could wish himself
in Thames up to the neck ; and so I would he were, and I
by him, at all adventures, so we were quit here.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
By my troth, I will speak my conscience of the king. I
think he would not wish himself anywhere but where he
is.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
By my troth, I will speak my conscience of the king. I
think he would not wish himself anywhere but where he
is.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
Then I would he were here alone; so should he be sure
to be ransomed, and a many poor men’s lives saved.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
I dare say you love him not so ill to wish him here
alone, howsoever you speak this to feel other men’s
minds. Methinks I could not die anywhere so contented as
in the king’s company, his cause being just and his
quarrel honorable.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
I dare say you love him not so ill to wish him here
alone, howsoever you speak this to feel other men’s
minds. Methinks I could not die anywhere so contented as
in the king’s company, his cause being just and his
quarrel honorable.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: That’s more than we know.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
Ay, or more than we should seek after, for we know
enough if we know we are the king’s subjects. If his
cause be wrong, our obedience to the king wipes the
crime of it out of us.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
Ay, or more than we should seek after, for we know
enough if we know we are the king’s subjects. If his
cause be wrong, our obedience to the king wipes the
crime of it out of us.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
But if the cause be not good, the king himself hath a
heavy reckoning to make, when all those legs and arms
and heads, chopped off in a battle, shall join together
at the latter day, and cry all, “We died at such a
place,” some swearing, some crying for a surgeon, some
upon their wives left poor behind them, some upon the
debts they owe, some upon their children rawly left. I
am afeard there are few die well that die in a battle,
for how can they charitably dispose of anything, when
blood is their argument? Now, if these men do not die
well, it will be a black matter for the king that led
them to it, who to disobey were against all proportion
of subjection.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
But if the cause be not good, the king himself hath a
heavy reckoning to make, when all those legs and arms
and heads, chopped off in a battle, shall join together
at the latter day, and cry all, “We died at such a

upon their wives left poor behind them, some upon the
debts they owe, some upon their children rawly left. I
am afeard there are few die well that die in a battle,
for how can they charitably dispose of anything, when
blood is their argument? Now, if these men do not die
well, it will be a black matter for the king that led
them to it, who to disobey were against all proportion
of subjection.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:

at the latter day, and cry all, “We died at such a

upon their wives left poor behind them, some upon the

am afeard there are few die well that die in a battle,
for how can they charitably dispose of anything, when

well, it will be a black matter for the king that led
them to it, who to disobey were against all proportion
of subjection.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
So, if a son that is by his father sent about
merchandise do sinfully miscarry upon the sea, the
imputation of his wickedness, by your rule, should be
imposed upon his father that sent him. Or if a servant,
under his master’s command transporting a sum of money,
be assailed by robbers and die in many irreconciled
iniquities, you may call the business of the master the
author of the servant’s damnation. But this is not so.
The king is not bound to answer the particular endings
of his soldiers, the father of his son, nor the master
of his servant, for they purpose not their death, when
they purpose their services. Besides, there is no king,
be his cause never so spotless, if it come to the
arbitrament of swords, can try it out with all unspotted
soldiers. Some, peradventure, have on them the guilt of
premeditated and contrived murder; some, of beguiling
virgins with the broken seals of perjury; some, making
the wars their bulwark, that have before gored the
gentle bosom of peace with pillage and robbery. Now, if
these men have defeated the law and outrun native
punishment, though they can outstrip men, they have no
wings to fly from God. War is His beadle, war is His
vengeance, so that here men are punished for
before-breach of the king’s laws in now the king’s
quarrel. Where they feared the death, they have borne
life away; and where they would be safe, they perish.
Then, if they die unprovided, no more is the king guilty
of their damnation than he was before guilty of those
impieties for the which they are now visited. Every
subject’s duty is the king’s, but every subject’s soul
is his own. Therefore should every soldier in the wars
do as every sick man in his bed: wash every mote out of
his conscience. And, dying so, death is to him
advantage; or not dying, the time was blessedly lost
wherein such preparation was gained. And in him that
escapes, it were not sin to think that, making God so
free an offer, He let him outlive that day to see His
greatness and to teach others how they should prepare.

of his soldiers, the father of his son, nor the master
of his servant, for they purpose not their death, when
they purpose their services. Besides, there is no king,
be his cause never so spotless, if it come to the
arbitrament of swords, can try it out with all unspotted
soldiers. Some, peradventure, have on them the guilt of
premeditated and contrived murder; some, of beguiling
virgins with the broken seals of perjury; some, making
the wars their bulwark, that have before gored the
gentle bosom of peace with pillage and robbery. Now, if
these men have defeated the law and outrun native
punishment, though they can outstrip men, they have no
wings to fly from God. War is His beadle, war is His
vengeance, so that here men are punished for
before-breach of the king’s laws in now the king’s
quarrel. Where they feared the death, they have borne
life away; and where they would be safe, they perish.
Then, if they die unprovided, no more is the king guilty
of their damnation than he was before guilty of those
impieties for the which they are now visited. Every
subject’s duty is the king’s, but every subject’s soul
is his own. Therefore should every soldier in the wars
do as every sick man in his bed: wash every mote out of
his conscience. And, dying so, death is to him
advantage; or not dying, the time was blessedly lost
wherein such preparation was gained. And in him that
escapes, it were not sin to think that, making God so
free an offer, He let him outlive that day to see His
greatness and to teach others how they should prepare.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
'Tis certain, every man that dies ill, the ill upon his
own head. The king is not to answer it.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
'Tis certain, every man that dies ill, the ill upon his
own head. The king is not to answer it.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
I do not desire he should answer for me, and yet I
determine to fight lustily for him.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: I myself heard the king say he would not be ransomed.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
Ay, he said so, to make us fight cheerfully, but when
our throats are cut, he may be ransomed, and we ne'er
the wiser.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: If I live to see it, I will never trust his word after.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
You pay him then. That’s a perilous shot out of an
elder gun, that a poor and private displeasure can do
against a monarch. You may as well go about to turn the
sun to ice with fanning in his face with a peacock’s
feather. You’ll “never trust his word after.” Come, ’tis
a foolish saying.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Your reproof is something too round. I should be angry
with you if the time were convenient.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Your reproof is something too round. I should be angry
with you if the time were convenient.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Let it be a quarrel between us, if you live.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: I embrace it.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: How shall I know thee again?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Give me any gage of thine, and I will wear it in my
bonnet. Then, if ever thou dar’st acknowledge it, I will
make it my quarrel.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Give me any gage of thine, and I will wear it in my
bonnet. Then, if ever thou dar’st acknowledge it, I will
make it my quarrel.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Here’s my glove. Give me another of thine.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Here’s my glove. Give me another of thine.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: There.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
This will I also wear in my cap. If ever thou come to
me and say, after tomorrow, “This is my glove,” by this
hand I will take thee a box on the ear.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text:
This will I also wear in my cap. If ever thou come to
me and say, after tomorrow, “This is my glove,” by this
hand I will take thee a box on the ear.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text: If ever I live to see it, I will challenge it.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Thou dar’st as well be hanged.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Well, I will do it, though I take thee in the king’s
company.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Keep thy word. Fare thee well.

sentences.speaker: WILLIAMS
sentences.text: Keep thy word. Fare thee well.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
Be friends, you English fools, be friends. We have
French quarrels enough, if you could tell how to reckon.

sentences.speaker: BATES
sentences.text:
Be friends, you English fools, be friends. We have
French quarrels enough, if you could tell how to reckon.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Indeed, the French may lay twenty French crowns to one
they will beat us, for they bear them on their
shoulders. But it is no English treason to cut French
crowns, and tomorrow the king himself will be a clipper.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Indeed, the French may lay twenty French crowns to one
they will beat us, for they bear them on their
shoulders. But it is no English treason to cut French
crowns, and tomorrow the king himself will be a clipper.
Exeunt soldiers

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Upon the king! Let us our lives, our souls, our debts,
our careful wives, our children, and our sins lay on the
king!
We must bear all. O hard condition,
Twin-born with greatness, subject to the breath
Of every fool, whose sense no more can feel
But his own wringing. What infinite heart’s ease
Must kings neglect that private men enjoy?
And what have kings that privates have not too,
Save ceremony, save general ceremony?
And what art thou, thou idol ceremony?
What kind of god art thou, that suffer’st more
Of mortal griefs than do thy worshippers?
What are thy rents? What are thy comings in?
O ceremony, show me but thy worth!
What is thy soul of adoration?
Art thou aught else but place, degree, and form,
Creating awe and fear in other men,
Wherein thou art less happy, being feared,
Than they in fearing?
What drink’st thou oft, instead of homage sweet,
But poisoned flattery? Oh, be sick, great greatness,
And bid thy ceremony give thee cure!
Think’st thou the fiery fever will go out
With titles blown from adulation?
Will it give place to flexure and low bending?
Canst thou, when thou command’st the beggar’s knee,
Command the health of it? No, thou proud dream,
That play’st so subtly with a king’s repose.
I am a king that find thee, and I know
'Tis not the balm, the scepter, and the ball,
The sword, the mace, the crown imperial,
The intertissued robe of gold and pearl,
The farcèd title running 'fore the king,
The throne he sits on, nor the tide of pomp
That beats upon the high shore of this world.
No, not all these, thrice-gorgeous ceremony,
Not all these, laid in bed majestical,
Can sleep so soundly as the wretched slave,
Who, with a body filled and vacant mind,
Gets him to rest, crammed with distressful bread;
Never sees horrid night, the child of hell,
But, like a lackey, from the rise to set
Sweats in the eye of Phoebus, and all night
Sleeps in Elysium; next day after dawn,
Doth rise and help Hyperion to his horse,
And follows so the ever-running year
With profitable labor to his grave.
And, but for ceremony, such a wretch,

Will it give place to flexure and low bending?
Canst thou, when thou command’st the beggar’s knee,
Command the health of it? No, thou proud dream,
That play’st so subtly with a king’s repose.
I am a king that find thee, and I know
'Tis not the balm, the scepter, and the ball,
The sword, the mace, the crown imperial,
The intertissued robe of gold and pearl,
The farcèd title running 'fore the king,
The throne he sits on, nor the tide of pomp
That beats upon the high shore of this world.
No, not all these, thrice-gorgeous ceremony,
Not all these, laid in bed majestical,
Can sleep so soundly as the wretched slave,
Who, with a body filled and vacant mind,
Gets him to rest, crammed with distressful bread;
Never sees horrid night, the child of hell,
But, like a lackey, from the rise to set
Sweats in the eye of Phoebus, and all night
Sleeps in Elysium; next day after dawn,
Doth rise and help Hyperion to his horse,
And follows so the ever-running year
With profitable labor to his grave.
And, but for ceremony, such a wretch,
Winding up days with toil and nights with sleep,
Had the forehand and vantage of a king.
The slave, a member of the country’s peace,
Enjoys it, but in gross brain little wots
What watch the king keeps to maintain the peace,
Whose hours the peasant best advantages.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Upon the king! Let us our lives, our souls, our debts,
our careful wives, our children, and our sins lay on the
king!
We must bear all. O hard condition,
Twin-born with greatness, subject to the breath
Of every fool, whose sense no more can feel
But his own wringing. What infinite heart’s ease
Must kings neglect that private men enjoy?
And what have kings that privates have not too,
Save ceremony, save general ceremony?
And what art thou, thou idol ceremony?
What kind of god art thou, that suffer’st more
Of mortal griefs than do thy worshippers?
What are thy rents? What are thy comings in?
O ceremony, show me but thy worth!
What is thy soul of adoration?
Art thou aught else but place, degree, and form,
Creating awe and fear in other men,
Wherein thou art less happy, being feared,
Than they in fearing?
What drink’st thou oft, instead of homage sweet,
But poisoned flattery? Oh, be sick, great greatness,
And bid thy ceremony give thee cure!
Think’st thou the fiery fever will go out
With titles blown from adulation?

Whose hours the peasant best advantages.

Enter ERPINGHAM

sentences.speaker: ERPINGHAM
sentences.text:
My lord, your nobles, jealous of your absence,
Seek through your camp to find you.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Good old knight,
Collect them all together at my tent.
I’ll be before thee.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
Good old knight,
Collect them all together at my tent.
I’ll be before thee.

sentences.speaker: ERPINGHAM
sentences.text: I shall do’t, my lord.
Exit

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
O God of battles, steel my soldiers' hearts.
Possess them not with fear. Take from them now
The sense of reck'ning ere th' opposèd numbers
Pluck their hearts from them. Not today, O Lord,
Oh, not today, think not upon the fault
My father made in compassing the crown.
I Richard’s body have interrèd anew,
And on it have bestowed more contrite tears
Than from it issued forcèd drops of blood.
Five hundred poor I have in yearly pay,
Who twice a day their withered hands hold up
Toward heaven to pardon blood. And I have built
Two chantries where the sad and solemn priests
Sing still for Richard’s soul. More will I do—
Though all that I can do is nothing worth,
Since that my penitence comes after all,
Imploring pardon.

Enter GLOUCESTER

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: My liege.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:
My brother Gloucester’s voice.—Ay,
I know thy errand. I will go with thee.
The day, my friends, and all things stay for me.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY
sentences.text:

Enter CONSTABLE

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text: Now, my Lord Constable?

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: Hark how our steeds for present service neigh.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: Hark how our steeds for present service neigh.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Mount them and make incision in their hides,
That their hot blood may spin in English eyes
And dout them with superfluous courage. Ha!

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Mount them and make incision in their hides,
That their hot blood may spin in English eyes
And dout them with superfluous courage. Ha!

sentences.speaker: RAMBURES
sentences.text:
What, will you have them weep our horses' blood?
How shall we then behold their natural tears?

sentences.speaker: RAMBURES
sentences.text:
What, will you have them weep our horses' blood?
How shall we then behold their natural tears?
Enter MESSENGER

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: The English are embattled, you French peers.

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
To horse, you gallant princes, straight to horse.
Do but behold yond poor and starvèd band,
And your fair show shall suck away their souls,
Leaving them but the shales and husks of men.
There is not work enough for all our hands,
Scarce blood enough in all their sickly veins
To give each naked curtal axe a stain,
That our French gallants shall today draw out
And sheathe for lack of sport. Let us but blow on them,
The vapor of our valor will o'erturn them.
'Tis positive against all exceptions, lords,
That our superfluous lackeys and our peasants,
Who in unnecessary action swarm
About our squares of battle, were enough
To purge this field of such a hilding foe,
Though we upon this mountain’s basis by
Took stand for idle speculation,
But that our honors must not. What’s to say?
A very little little let us do,
And all is done. Then let the trumpets sound
The tucket sonance and the note to mount,
For our approach shall so much dare the field
That England shall couch down in fear and yield.

'Tis positive against all exceptions, lords,
That our superfluous lackeys and our peasants,
Who in unnecessary action swarm
About our squares of battle, were enough
To purge this field of such a hilding foe,
Though we upon this mountain’s basis by
Took stand for idle speculation,
But that our honors must not. What’s to say?
A very little little let us do,
And all is done. Then let the trumpets sound
The tucket sonance and the note to mount,
For our approach shall so much dare the field
That England shall couch down in fear and yield.
Enter GRANDPRÉ

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text: They have said their prayers, and they stay for death.

sentences.speaker: DAUPHIN
sentences.text:
Shall we go send them dinners and fresh suits,
And give their fasting horses provender,
And after fight with them?

sentences.speaker: CONSTABLE
sentences.text:
I stay but for my guard. On, to the field!
I will the banner from a trumpet take
And use it for my haste. Come, come away.
The sun is high, and we outwear the day.

Exeunt
Enter GLOUCESTER, BEDFORD, EXETER, ERPINGHAM, with all his host, SALISBURY, and WESTMORELAND

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: Where is the king?

sentences.speaker: BEDFORD
sentences.text: The king himself is rode to view their battle.

sentences.speaker: WESTMORELAND
sentences.text: Of fighting men they have full threescore thousand.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text: There’s five to one. Besides, they all are fresh.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text: There’s five to one. Besides, they all are fresh.

sentences.speaker: SALISBURY
sentences.text:
God’s arm strike with us! 'Tis a fearful odds.
God be wi' you, princes all. I’ll to my charge.
If we no more meet till we meet in heaven,
Then joyfully, my noble Lord of Bedford,
My dear Lord Gloucester, and my good Lord Exeter,
And my kind kinsman, warriors all, adieu.

sentences.speaker: BEDFORD
sentences.text: Farewell, good Salisbury, and good luck go with thee.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text:
Farewell, kind lord. Fight valiantly today.
And yet I do thee wrong to mind thee of it,
For thou art framed of the firm truth of valor.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text:

And yet I do thee wrong to mind thee of it,
For thou art framed of the firm truth of valor.
